does: This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark of 847 adversarial test cases for evaluating prompt injection vulnerabilities in retrieval-augmented generation systems across five attack categories: direct injection, context manipulation, instruction override, data exfiltration, and cross-context contamination. The authors propose a multi-layered defense framework combining content filtering with embedding-based anomaly detection, hierarchical system prompt guardrails, and multi-stage response verification. They evaluate all three mechanisms across seven state-of-the-art language models, demonstrating that their combined approach reduces successful attack rates from 73.2% to 8.7% while preserving 94.3% of baseline task performance. The work provides both the evaluation benchmark and open-source implementations to facilitate future research.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about prompt injection attacks in language model systems, extending prior research that identified basic prompt injection vulnerabilities to the more complex setting of RAG-augmented agents. It connects to earlier work on adversarial attacks against neural language models and defenses like prompt engineering and input filtering, while also drawing from research on system prompt design and verification methods in AI safety. The embedding-based anomaly detection approach relates to prior work in detecting out-of-distribution inputs, and the multi-stage verification concept draws from defense-in-depth principles established in cybersecurity and more recent AI robustness literature.
enables: This comprehensive benchmark and defense framework enable future research to systematically evaluate new attack and defense methods against RAG systems at scale. The released dataset and implementation support the development of more sophisticated defense mechanisms, such as adaptive filtering that learns from attack patterns or verification approaches using external knowledge sources. The work points toward understanding how language models can be made robust to adversarial inputs while maintaining utility, laying groundwork for deploying RAG agents in high-stakes domains. It also opens directions for studying the fundamental tension between system prompt flexibility and security, and developing certifiable defenses for language model-based systems.
